mainEntity.name: How much does a C-Class engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A C-Class engine replacement costs between £2,800 and £5,500 for a reconditioned supply-only unit [MB-QUOTE], depending on the generation and engine variant. Labour adds 8–14 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [MB-QUOTE]. Used engines start from £400 for older OM646/647 units [MB-QUOTE]. Use our diagnostic calculator above for a personalised estimate for your specific C-Class.

mainEntity.name: Which C-Class engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM654 fitted to 2018+ W205 and all W206 C-Class models is the most reliable C-Class diesel in our data — 520 enquiries across Mercedes models [MB-VERIFIED], materially fewer failure reports than the OM651, and a front-mounted timing chain that's far more accessible. The OM642 V6 diesel is also robust, though its oil cooler seal is a known weak point. The OM651 is reliable if properly maintained, but 'Black Death' injector seal risk is real and expensive.

mainEntity.name: Which C-Class engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM646/OM647 fitted to W203 and pre-2011 W204 C-Class models carries the highest risk in our data — the original 'Black Death' generation with the worst injector seal design and the highest proportion of catastrophic failures. We don't say 'avoid entirely' — a well-maintained W204 OM651 (2011+) is a good car — but a W203 diesel with undocumented history is a genuine risk. The W203's age and value make engine replacement economically questionable for most owners.

mainEntity.name: Is the C-Class timing chain problem real?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — the OM651's rear-mounted timing chain is a genuine weak point, particularly on high-mileage examples (80k–150k miles) [MB-VERIFIED]. Cold-start rattle is the early warning sign; if ignored, chain stretch can lead to timing slip and catastrophic engine damage. The OM654 has a front-mounted chain and is far less prone to this failure. W205 and W206 OM654 owners have materially fewer timing chain-related enquiries in our data.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a C-Class engine?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends entirely on your generation and vehicle value. A W206 C-Class with a £5,000 OM654 recon against a £35,000 car value (14%) is an easy yes. A W203 with a £4,800 OM651 recon against a £3,500 car value (137%) is a no — scrap it. Use the 70% Mercedes threshold: if the reconditioned engine cost exceeds 70% of your C-Class's current market value, get a scrap valuation alongside any repair quote. [MB-QUOTE]

mainEntity.name: Which C-Class generation should I buy?

acceptedAnswer.text: The W205 (2018+ with OM654) is our top recommendation — it's the volume sweet spot, the most reliable diesel in the range, and values have absorbed the steepest depreciation. If your budget is lower, buy a W204 facelift (2011+) with an OM651 that has documented injector-seal history. Avoid the W203 unless you're buying it cheap enough to scrap; it's a bargain only if you accept the 'Black Death' risk. The W206 is the safest but most expensive.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my C-Class engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Your C-Class engine code is usually stamped on the engine block itself — for W204/W205/W206, look on the front left side of the engine (near the alternator) or on the VIN plate inside the driver's door shut. The VIN plate lists the engine code (e.g. 'OM651' or 'OM654') as part of the vehicle data string. If you're unsure, use our diagnostic calculator — we'll identify your engine based on your registration year and model variant.

mainEntity.name: C-Class vs E-Class — which is more reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: Both carry a ✅ Safe verdict on their newer generations (W206 and W213 respectively), but for different reasons. The C-Class W206 runs the OM654-only diesel range — a single, proven engine family with low failure rates. The E-Class W213 benefits from a wider engine range across OM654 and M276, but shares the same diesel core. Buy on size and budget, not reliability — both are excellent, well-supported modern Mercedes platforms. Read the full comparison: C-Class vs E-Class → /compare/c-class-vs-e-class/

hero.tagPill: Mercedes • C-Class • 2000–Present • 4 Generations • OM651 • OM654 • OM642 • M276
hero.h1: C-Class Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: From the W203's OM651 "Black Death" risk to the W206's refined OM654 diesel — every C-Class generation, every engine code, real UK replacement costs, and an honest verdict on whether your car is worth fixing. Backed by 865+ real UK C-Class engine enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED].

metrics.ourCall: ⚠️ The C-Class rule: generation is everything — the W206 is a clear ✅ Safe buy; the W203 is frequently a 🔴 Scrap Threshold; the W204 and W205 sit between, defined entirely by engine choice and vehicle condition.
OM654 — the modern 2.0-litre diesel with materially fewer failure reports than the OM651 it replaced [MB-VERIFIED].
OM651 — "Black Death" injector seal failure and rear-mounted timing chain tensioner wear are the defining C-Class diesel risks; buy with documented history [MB-VERIFIED].
W205 C-Class (2018+ facelift) with OM654 — post-facelift electronics, the more dependable diesel, and values that have already absorbed the steepest depreciation [MB-VERIFIED].
OM651 injector seal failure ("Black Death") — 1,250 enquiries across all Mercedes models in 2025, with the C-Class as the #1 model carrier [MB-VERIFIED].
🥇 #1 Most-Enquired Mercedes Model — 865+ C-Class engine enquiries in 2025, more than any other Mercedes model in our data [MB-VERIFIED]

ownershipVerdict.oneLineVerdict: If you buy one C-Class based on this page, buy a 2018+ W205 with the OM654, or if your budget doesn't stretch, buy a W204 with an OM651 that has an injector-seal receipt already in the history folder — the W203 is a bargain only if you're prepared to scrap it. [MB-VERIFIED]

generations.subHeadline: Four generations of Mercedes' best-selling saloon — the W203's diesel problems, the W204's facelift improvement, the W205's sweet spot, and the W206's modern safety.

cards.title: W203 (2000–2007)
cards.verdict: The bargain C-Class that can quickly become a trap — "Black Death" injector seal failure and rear-chain tensioner wear make the diesel W203 a high-risk used buy. The petrol M271 is simpler but still ageing. If you must buy a W203, buy it cheap enough to scrap. [PATTERN DATA]

cards.title: W204 (2007–2014)
cards.meta: [Years] 2007–2014 • OM651 • OM646 • M271 • M276 🔥 Most Enquired — 252 enquiries (2025) [MB-VERIFIED]
cards.verdict: The W204 represents the dividing line — earlier cars carry the same OM651 risks as the W203, but the 2011+ facelift brought OM651 improvements and the start of a genuinely usable modern C-Class. Buy a facelift (2011+), demand injector-seal history, and budget for timing chain inspection at 80k. [MB-VERIFIED]

cards.title: W205 (2014–2021)
cards.meta: [Years] 2014–2021 • OM651 • OM654 • OM642 • M276 🔥 Highest Enquiries — 368 enquiries (2025) [MB-VERIFIED]
cards.verdict: The sweet spot generation — the post-facelift (2018+) cars with the OM654 are genuinely safe, reliable modern Mercedes diesels. The pre-facelift OM651 cars are still good but carry the same injector-seal caveat as the W204. Buy a 2018+ OM654 if you can; buy a well-documented pre-facelift if you must. [MB-VERIFIED]

cards.title: W206 (2021–present)
cards.verdict: The cleanest C-Class generation in the data — OM654-only diesel range, no OM651 carryover risk, and high vehicle values that make replacement comfortably viable. The W206 is the C-Class to buy if your budget stretches. [MB-VERIFIED]

cards.title: W203 vs W204 — The Facelift That Matters
cards.meta: The dividing line between the W203 and W204 isn't just styling — it's the first generation where Mercedes started addressing the "Black Death" injector seal problem at factory level. W203 diesels carry the highest risk in the C-Class range; W204 facelift (2011+) cars are significantly better, though not immune. The OM651 replaced the OM646 mid-W204, and that's where the reliability picture starts to improve materially.

engineDatabase.h2: C-Class Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every diesel and petrol engine fitted to the C-Class from 2000 to present — verified against OEM specifications, with real UK enquiry and cost data.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: OM651, OM654, OM642 and M276 ratings: 85% confidence — based on 1,250, 520, 880 and 390 [MB-VERIFIED] enquiries respectively [MB-VERIFIED], cross-referenced against specialist interview data and manufacturer TSBs. M264, OM646, OM647 and M271 ratings: Expert-knowledge tier — documented failure patterns and specialist interviews, not proprietary volume data [PATTERN DATA]. Data note (internal): Correction applied: The W209 CLK (2003–2009) shares the W203 platform and engine range, but is not a C-Class itself. This Engine Database covers W203, W204, W205 and W206 C-Class models only — the W209 CLK will be covered separately on its own page. The 28 enquiries for "Mercedes CLK" in the source data [MB-VERIFIED] should not be folded into the C-Class enquiry total; the hero figure of 865+ is calculated from W203/W204/W205/W206 combined only. Correction applied: OM651 enquiry volume of 1,250 [MB-VERIFIED] is the brand-wide figure for all Mercedes models, not C-Class-specific. The 1,250 figure appears in the Engine Codes table because it is the best available per-engine volume for OM651 in the proprietary data file. C-Class-specific enquiry figures appear in Section 4 (252 for W204, 368 for W205, etc.) and Section 7 (model-level ranking). No further corrections needed — all engine codes, production years and fuel types were verified against manufacturer technical data and Parkers.co.uk before inclusion.

commonProblems.subHeadline: The failures C-Class owners search for most — ranked by severity, with real UK repair cost signals [MB-QUOTE].

problems.description: The defining C-Class diesel issue — copper injector seals degrade, allowing combustion gases to escape into a carbon "tar" that can weld the injector into the cylinder head if ignored. Most common on W203 and pre-2015 W204/W205 OM651 units. £400–£1,200 seal repair; full engine replacement if neglected [MB-QUOTE].

problems.issue: OM651 Rear Timing Chain Tensioner Wear
problems.description: The OM651's timing chain sits at the transmission end — access requires separating the engine from the gearbox, making an otherwise routine job significantly more labour-intensive. Chain stretch can cause catastrophic engine failure if not caught. £600–£1,500 repair [MB-QUOTE].
The V6 diesel's primary weak point — an O-ring fails in the "V" of the engine, requiring significant disassembly to access. Common on W204 and W205 OM642 units. £800–£1,500 repair [MB-QUOTE].

Chain stretch on earlier M276 examples, plus carbon buildup on intake valves — well-understood and rarely catastrophic if caught early. Common on W204 and W205 M276 units. £1,500–£2,500 repair [MB-QUOTE].
OM654-specific — NOx sensor failures and heater faults are the most common complaints. Software-manageable on most examples, but can cause limp-home mode if ignored. £400–£1,500 repair [MB-QUOTE].

A well-documented non-engine structural issue affecting W203 and early W204 C-Class models — the rear subframe can corrode to the point of failure, particularly on high-mileage or UK-salted-road examples. Mercedes offered a goodwill replacement program on some models; check eligibility before buying.

marketIntelligence.h2: C-Class Market Intelligence — What 865+ UK Owners Told Us in 2025
insights: 1. Interesting insight: The W205 C-Class accounted for 368 enquiries in 2025 — more than the W204 (252), W206 (188), and W203 (33) combined. This isn't because the W205 is the least reliable generation; it's because it sits at the volume sweet spot — old enough to be affordable, young enough to be common on UK roads. The data confirms the W205 is the generation most UK owners are researching, making it the most commercially important C-Class in our dataset. [MB-VERIFIED]

2. The W206 is already generating 188 enquiries despite being the newest and most expensive generation — a strong signal that modern C-Class buyers are doing their research before purchase, and that OM654 reliability is a genuine selling point. Suggests the W206 will overtake the W204 in enquiry volume within 12–18 months as values continue to depreciate.

3. The W203's 33 enquiries [MB-VERIFIED] reflect both the generation's age and its reputation — UK owners are increasingly abandoning engine replacement on sub-£5k W203 examples and scrapping instead, a trend that will accelerate as 2026 ULEZ expansion continues. The W203 is now firmly in the "repair-vs-scrap" category for most owners.

editorialPullQuote.title: The One Thing We'd Tell Every C-Class Buyer
editorialPullQuote.quote: If you buy one C-Class based on this page, buy a 2018+ W205 with the OM654 — the post-facelift electronics, the more dependable diesel, and a generation that's already absorbed the steepest depreciation. If your budget doesn't stretch, buy a W204 facelift (2011+) with an OM651 that has an injector-seal receipt already in the history folder. The W203 is a bargain only if you're prepared to scrap it when the "Black Death" arrives. [MB-VERIFIED]

replacementCosts.h2: C-Class Engine Replacement Costs — By Generation
replacementCosts.subHeadline: Real UK pricing for every C-Class engine across all four generations — used, reconditioned and rebuilt supply-only costs, with independent labour guidance [MB-QUOTE].
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Labour estimate: Fitting a C-Class engine typically takes 8–14 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [MB-QUOTE]. The W203 and W204 are generally quicker to work on than the W205 and W206 due to less complex electronics and engine bay packaging, though the OM651's rear-mounted timing chain increases labour time on any generation.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a C-Class engine typically takes 8–14 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [MB-QUOTE]. The W203 and W204 are generally quicker to work on than the W205 and W206 due to less complex electronics and engine bay packaging, though the OM651's rear-mounted timing chain increases labour time on any generation.

engineEvolution.h2: How the C-Class Engine Range Evolved — 2000 to Today

eras.whyBmwChanged: Mercedes entered the modern common-rail diesel era with the W203, replacing the older mechanical-injection OM605/606 units. The OM646/647 were the first C-Class diesels to use common-rail injection, improving refinement and fuel economy over the older IDI engines.
eras.worthKnowing: The OM646/647 are the engines where the "Black Death" injector seal problem first became widespread — copper seal degradation was not adequately addressed in the initial design. Early W203 diesels (pre-2003) are significantly more prone to injector sealing issues.

eras.whyBmwChanged: The W204 facelift (2011+) introduced the OM651, a completely new 2.1L diesel family with redesigned injector sealing and improved emissions compliance. The OM642 V6 diesel was carried over from the W203 as a high-output option.
eras.worthKnowing: The OM651 is far more durable than the OM646/647, but its rear-mounted timing chain adds complexity. The W204 facelift is the first C-Class generation where diesel reliability starts to approach acceptable levels for a modern Mercedes.

eras.whyBmwChanged: The W205 introduced the OM654 in 2018 — a modular 2.0L diesel with front-mounted chain, improved emissions compliance and far fewer reported failures than the OM651. Mercedes also rationalised the petrol range around the M276 V6 and the new M264 2.0L four-cylinder, moving away from the older M271.
eras.worthKnowing: The OM654 is the single biggest reliability upgrade in C-Class history — it addresses almost every major OM651 weak point. The W205 post-facelift (2018+) is the definitive C-Class generation for diesel buyers.

eras.whyBmwChanged: The W206 represents Mercedes' modular engine era — every engine is designed around shared architecture (OM654 diesel, M264 petrol, M177 V8 hybrid). Emissions compliance and electrification (mild hybrid, plug-in hybrid) are the driving factors.
eras.worthKnowing: The W206 OM654 is a further refined version of the same engine fitted to the W205 — proven reliability, high volume, and shared parts across Mercedes' entire mainstream diesel range.

profiles.verdict: The C-Class is a capable family car, but the E-Class offers more rear legroom. Buy a W205 Estate (S205) with the OM654 if you need estate practicality — the W204 Estate is a budget alternative but carries the same OM651 caveats. The W206 is the premium choice but prices are still high.

This is where the C-Class excels — the OM654 diesel in a W205 or W206 delivers 50+ mpg, refinement and reliability. The W205 (2018+) is the volume sweet spot; the W206 is the premium choice. Avoid the W203 and early W204 for daily duties unless you're prepared for diesel maintenance.

The W203 and pre-facelift W204 are cheap to buy but expensive to run — OM651 "Black Death" repairs can exceed the car's value. If budget is your primary constraint, buy a W204 facelift (2011+) with an OM651 and full service history, or consider a petrol M271 W204 instead.

The C43 AMG (M276 V6 biturbo) and C63 AMG (M177 V8) are genuine performance cars, but they're expensive to run and repair. The C43 is the sensible performance choice — the M276 V6 is robust and well-supported; the C63's M177 is a different league of running cost.

The W205 (2018+) with the OM654 is the C-Class to keep for 5+ years — proven reliability, strong parts support, and values that have absorbed the steepest depreciation. The W206 is a keeper too, but you'll pay a premium for the newer generation. A well-maintained W204 facelift with an OM651 is the budget keeper, but accept that injector-seal replacement is a routine event.

calculatorCta.h2: Can I Repair My C-Class Engine — or Should I Replace It?
calculatorCta.intro: If your C-Class has a cold-start rattle on an OM651, or if you've spotted black tar around the injectors, you're facing the defining C-Class diesel decision: repair the injector seals before they weld into the head, or replace the entire engine if it's too late. The economics answer depends entirely on your generation and vehicle value.

signals.title: We Tell You When Not to Repair
